coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as metal-free photocatalyst for dye degradation with high efficiency within nine minutes in dark
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuai
Fan, Xiaoyu
Guan, Rentian
Hu, Yingying
Jiang, Shuhan
Shao, Xiaodong
Wang, Shuhao
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: A metal-free nanocatalyst was proposed to be applied in the photocatalysis of dye degradation based on carbon dots (CDs). CDs show excellent catalytic performance with higher photocatalytic efficiency and shorter degradation time than those for most of previous works. CDs were obtained by a simple one-pot solvothermal method using glyoxal and ethanol as precursor materials with quantum yield (0.26). The morphological, structural, and optical properties and functional groups for CDs were characterized by numerous techniques. The decolorization efficiency of IC can get to 91% in the sun, indoor, and in dark. Furthermore, the degradation time is 4.5 min in the sun, which is shorter than other reports. The degradation rate constant is 0.553 min−1 which is about 10 times higher than most of previous works. The present CDs as nanocatalysts may be explored to degrade more dyes or other organic molecules in environmental wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Indigo carmine
Photocatalytic activity
Degradation time

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of CDs measurement
Effect of reaction temperature, time and molar ratio of precursors
Effect of the concentration of CDs, hydrogen peroxide and IC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectrum (a), FT-IR spectrum (b), TEM images (c), and the corresponding size distribution (d) of CDs.
XPS full spectrum (a), HR-XPS of C1s (b) and O1s (c) of CDs.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (a), digital photograph of the CDs solution irradiated under roomlight (insert left) and under UV light (insert right), with the change on excitation wavelength, respectively (b), and quinine sulfate (QS) as reference to measure the QY of CDs (c).

Plot of (Ct/C0) for IC photodegradation by CDs under different conditions (a), the corresponding plot of ln (Ct/C0) vs. time of IC with CDs (b), number of cycles for the IC to catalytic degradation over CDs (c), and digital images of IC solutions under different degradation times with (d) or without sunlight (e) and dark (f), respectively.
Schematic illustration for the catalytic degradation mechanism of CDs on organic dyes.
Degradation of IC over CDs with or without the scavenger under with (a) or without sunlight (b) and dark (c), respectively, (Concentration: BQ 0.5 mM, IPA 0.196 M, Na2-EDTA 0.5 mM).
Synthesis of CDs and the degradation process of IC.
Results of dye degradation efficiency with different materials.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as metal-free photocatalyst for dye degradation with high efficiency within nine minutes in dark

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Energy Storage and Novel Cell Technology, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian, 710077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian, 710077, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A metal-free nanocatalyst was proposed to be applied in the photocatalysis of dye degradation based on carbon dots (CDs). CDs show excellent catalytic performance with higher photocatalytic efficiency and shorter degradation time than those for most of previous works. CDs were obtained by a simple one-pot solvothermal method using glyoxal and ethanol as precursor materials with quantum yield (0.26). The morphological, structural, and optical properties and functional groups for CDs were characterized by numerous techniques. The decolorization efficiency of IC can get to 91% in the sun, indoor, and in dark. Furthermore, the degradation time is 4.5 min in the sun, which is shorter than other reports. The degradation rate constant is 0.553 min−1 which is about 10 times higher than most of previous works. The present CDs as nanocatalysts may be explored to degrade more dyes or other organic molecules in environmental wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of modern industry, environmental problems are more and more serious, especially for water pollution [1]. Although organic dyes are necessary and widely used in modern industries, there are severe water pollution ascribing from their excessive use and discharge without thorough treatment [2,3]. The continuous increase of wastewater will result in the severe water scarcity in the near future, which is urgent to explore an efficient approach to remove contaminants and ameliorate wastewater [4,5].

Photocatalysis is a kind of catalysis that bring about the change of the rate of a photoreaction after the absorption of light by one or more reacting species by adding photocatalyst or photosensitizer [6,7]. Considerable photocatalysts have been developed for the degradation of contaminants in water including organic dyes and polyaromatic hydrocarbons [8]. Various metal-containing photocatalysts were employed for dye degradation such as metal-based materials including TiO2 [9], ZnO [10], ZnO–Bi2O3 [11], BiPO4/Fe-metal organic framework composite [12], Bi5Ti3FeO15 [13], Eu-TiO2GO [14], GO/Gd/ZnO [15] and ZrO2 [16], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [17], and Fe-based glassy powder [18]. Nevertheless, all of the photocatalysts contain at least one metal as a major component, considering the large-scale production which may raise economic cost [19,20]. Therefore, it is a challenge for the exploration of low cost metal-free photocatalysts with excellent visible light harvesting and high photocatalysis [21–24]. Metal-free photocatalysts have attracted much attention, which may be used for molecular oxygen activation and pollutant degradation [25–27]. Among the nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of nanophotocatalysts, show many advantages such as super light-harvesting, excellent photostability, high catalytic efficiency, low cost and toxicity [16,28]. The above CDs, however, are generally explored to conjunct predominantly with other metallic photosensitizers. In this way, there are some difficulties to explain clearly the photoreactivty of CDs. In fact, CDs alone can show a high photocatalytic activity for photoreduction of organic dye [29].

As a photocatalysis reaction for the dye degradation, the degradation efficiency and time is especially important. For various dyes, different photocatalysts show different results in previous works. For Acid Scarlet 3R dye, Fe doped TiO2 exhibits about 91.15% [30], TiO2 composite is about 92% [31], CoSO4 is about 97% [32], Fe-based composite is about 96.6% [33], and silica supported copper is about 97% [34], respectively. For methyl red, the decolorization is about 70% using ZnO film [10], about 93% [35], 96.3% [36], 86% [37], 94% [38] using Mn2+/Mn7+-TiO2 nanocomposites, Co3O4-0.5RHA, Co3O4 nanoparticles, and g-C3N4/MnV2O6, respectively. The degradation time for most works, however, is more than 120 min. Furthermore, for the dye degradation it is significant as well whether the toxicity of products is lower than that of the dye molecule. A few works considered the products of dye degradation [39].

In this work, CDs as free-metal nanomaterials are proposed to apply in the photocatalysis of dye degradation. CDs are prepared from glyoxal and ethanol using a simple solvothermal method. Indigo carmine (IC) as a model dye is used to investigate the catalytic activity of CDs. Under the sun light outdoor, the photocatalytic efficiency arrives at 91.2% within 4.5 min with the auxiliary of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The decolorization is also tested indoor without direct sun light and in dark. It is interesting that the efficiency reaches 91.5% within 9 min for both indoor and in dark. By comparison with previous works, the present CDs products show excellent photocatalytic efficiency and shortest time. The present CDs as nanocatalysts may be explored to degrade more dyes or other organic molecules in environmental wastewater.

In order to record the crystalline phase information, the D/Max-2500 Diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) finished powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements. FT-IR spectra were recorded using the Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). The morphology of CDs was performed by a TEM images (JEM-2100F) (JEOL Ltd., Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were undertaken with a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectra (HNMR) were obtained on a AVANCE NEO 500 (BRUKER, Germany). UV–Vis absorption were recorded using the UH4150 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) were recorded using the F-7100 spectrophotometer (HITACH, Japan).A G2-XS mass spectrometer (Waters Co., Ltd, USA) was used to measure mass spectra of CDs.

Glyoxal solution, 40 wt % in H2O (8.8 M), IC and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were ordered from Shanghai PERFEMIKER Material Tech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (≥99.7%) were purchased from Yantai Far Eastern Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Yantai, China) ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2-EDTA) were purchased from Damao chemical reagent factory (Tianjin, China). Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) (≥99.7%) was purchased from Tianjin Guabgfu Sci and Tech Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China), and benzoquinone (BQ) was ordered from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). All reagents used in relevant experiments were analytical grade and without further purification. Deionized water supplied by the Milli-Q water Purified system was used in all experiments.

The synthesis steps are shown in Scheme 1 . The CDs were synthesized based on solvothermal method by reference to a literature with some changes [40]. Briefly, glyoxal and ethanol solutions (with molar ratio of 2:3) were transferred to Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, some brown solutions of CDs were obtained. For further characterization, 1 mL CDs solution was heated in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 24 h, from which 294.6 mg solid CDs was obtained. The CDs products were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for latter use.

The catalytic activity of CDs in the presence of H2O2 was studied for the degradation of IC with or without sunlight, and in dark. 30 μL CDs (8.85 mg/mL) and 2 μL H2O2 (20 mM) were mixed with 970 μL IC solution (0.04 mg/mL). After mixing, the UV–vis absorption spectra of IC were recorded with regular time intervals. In addition, a control experiment for IC degradation was also carried out under the same condition. The maximum absorbance at 610 nm for IC was used to analyze the degradation of IC.

The catalytic activities were investigated through the degradation of IC. Analytical samples of IC solutions were analyzed at 0.5 min or 1 min intervals with or without sunlight, and in dark, respectively. The remaining concentration of IC after degradation was determined by the UV–vis absorption spectrum.
Degradation efficiency
( % )
=
[
(
A 0
- A
t
)
/A 0
]
× 100 %
Where A
0 represents the absorbance at 610 nm before degradation, A
t are the absorbance at the same wavelength after degradation, for time t, respectively.
The reactive species caught up in the catalytic degradation of IC were determined using appropriate scavengers. Na2-EDTA, BQ, and IPA were used as scavengers for h+, O2·- and OH·, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of CDs measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY 54%). The QY of CDs was calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
Q X
= Q
ST
(
K X
/K ST
)
(
η 2
X/
η 2
ST )

In eq. (1), Q, K, X, ST and η are the quantum yield, the gradient in the curve of the relationship, the reference (quinine sulfate), the test samples (CDs) and refractive index of the solvent, respectively.

The morphological, structural, functional groups and optical properties for CDs were characterized by multiple techniques, which were XRD, FT-IR, TEM, XPS, and fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry, respectively.

The XRD pattern of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. A broad XRD pattern of CDs appears at 2θ = 22.5°, which corresponds to the graphite structure lattice spacing of (002). The FT-IR spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 1b. It can be indicated that 3440 cm−1 and 2980 cm−1 for O–H and –CH3 stretching. The band at1746 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of the CO and CC. The absorption peak of 1116 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the C–O–C. The morphology and particle size of CDs can be seen from the TEM image of Fig. 1c and d, which are dispersive without distinct aggregation. The particle size distributions of CDs can be gained from the TEM image by counting more than 100 nanoparticles, and the average size was 2.5 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 1d). The chemical composition and chemical bonding of the CDs were studied using the XPS. Fig. 2 a shows two main peaks at 533.09 and 287.87 eV in the XPS spectrum, which are ascribed to O1s and C1s. So, O and C elements exist in the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, C1s shows four characteristic peaks at 285 eV (C–C/CC), 286.5 eV (C–O) and 288.1 eV (CO). Three main peaks located at 532.9 eV and 533.5 eV in Fig. 2c, illustrating that CDs own C–O–C and C–O groups. In addition, to prove whether there are aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs, HNMR was carried out as shown in Fig. S1 (in Supporting Information). It can be found that there are aldehyde, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups in the structure of CDs, which consist with the results of XPS.

The UV–Vis absorption and FL spectra were used to explain optical properties of CDs. Fig. 3 a shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs and there is an absorption at 267 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of CC functional groups. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength values are 386 nm and 490 nm, respectively. CDs show photoluminescence features depending on excitation wavelength (Fig. 3b). The emission peaks shift to red side with the increase of excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity gets to the maximum at 386 nm. Fig. 3c exhibits the fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of absorbance using quinine sulfate (QS) as reference. After calculation, the QY of CDs is about 0.26.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature, time and molar ratio of precursors
ce:section.ce:para: To attain the optimum catalytic performance, some synthesis conditions of CDs were research, such as reaction time, temperature and molar ratio. The synthesis steps of CDs are listed in Scheme 1. Fig. S2a (in Supporting Information) indicates the influence of reaction time on the catalytic performance of CDs. When the heating time varies from 0.5 h to 4 h, the value of Ct/C0 goes down and up and gets to the minimum at 2 h. Fig. S2b (in Supporting Information) illustrates the influence of reaction temperature on the catalytic activity of CDs. When the heating temperature changes from 130 °C to 170 °C, the minimum value of Ct/C0 appears at 160 °C. Fig. S2c (in Supporting Information) displays the effect of the molar ratio of precursors (glyoxal:ethanol) on the value of Ct/C0. Four experiments were carried out with the molar ratio of glyoxal:ethanol varying from 1:1, 2:3, 1:3 to 1:4, respectively. It can be found that the minimum value of Ct/C0 appears when the molar ratio arrives to 2:3. Therefore, CDs were prepared at 160 °C for 2 h with molar ratio (glyoxal:ethanol) of 2:3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration of CDs, hydrogen peroxide and IC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the best catalytic performance of CDs, the concentration of CDs, H2O2 and IC was measured, respectively. Fig. S3a (in Supporting Information) shows the effect of concentration of CDs on the value of Ct/C0. The ratio (Ct/C0) arrives at the minimum with the addition of 30 μL (8.85 mg/mL) CDs when the volume of CDs changes from 5 μL to 40 μL. The influence of H2O2 was also investigated by comparison of the ratio of Ct/C0 (Fig. S3b, in Supporting Information). It can be observed that the ratio of Ct/C0 gets to the minimum when the concentration of H2O2 gets to 20 mM with the concentration varying from 10 mM to 100 mM. It can be deduced that it is not the best for the catalytic activity of CDs using the highest concentration of H2O2. It was reported that the superfluous H2O2 can trap OH· to form a weaker oxidant ·HO2 [41]. To estimate the catalytic performance of CDs, the influence of IC concentration was also researched (Fig. S4, in Supporting Information). The concentration of IC was analyzed by the UV–vis absorption spectrometry. By comparison, the ratio of Ct/C0 is lower using IC concentration at 0.04 mg/mL than that at 0.05 and 0.06 mg/mL IC. Therefore, 30 μL CDs (8.85 mg/mL) and 2 μL H2O2 (20 mM) were mixed with 970 μL IC solution (0.04 mg/mL).

The proposed findings involve new applications of CDs. In addition to the well-known conventional applications of CDs, it is also used to degrade IC with or without sunlight, and in dark (Fig. 4 ). As expected, IC alone cannot be degraded in the sun, and there is a slight degradation of IC in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 4a). There is obvious degradation of IC with photocatalysis of CDs while the reaction rate is low. It is interesting that H2O2 can significantly accelerate the process of IC degradation even in dark. More importantly, the change of Ct/C0 for IC degradation with time is highest using CDs-H2O2 system in the sun, which is basically steady within 4.5 min with the efficiency at 91.2%. The reaction rate is similar indoor and in dark, and the process of degradation can complete within 9 min with the efficiency at 91.5%. The degradation efficiency of IC is similar for the three experiments. It can be deduced that the catalytic activity of CDs can release effectively in the presence of H2O2. Furthermore, IC can be degraded easily using the system of CDs-H2O2 even without light. The rate of photocatalysis is improved twice with the sunlight by comparison with those without sunlight and in dark.

Fig. 4b shows the corresponding plot of ln (Ct/C0) vs. time for IC degradation. It suggests that the photocatalytic reaction of IC can be fitted with the Langmuir-Hinschelwood model. The rate constant can be obtained according to the apparent first-order kinetic model, which were represented as ln(C0/Ct) = kt. Where C0 represents the initial concentration of dye, Ct is the concentration of dye at the time (t), and k represents the reaction rate constant. The results show that the degradation of IC accords with the apparent first-order kinetics, and the degradation rate constant values can be calculated as 0.553 min−1, 0.2258 min−1, and 0.2432 min−1 with and without sunlight, and in dark, respectively.

The reusability of the CDs-H2O2 system was also tested by supplement of CDs without separation. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the catalytic performance of the CDs-H2O2 can be used repeatedly for 9 cycle times. It is obvious that the degradation efficiency of IC decreases slightly after each cycle. After 9 cycle times, the degradation efficiency of CDs still maintains 83%. It may be attributed to the loss of H2O2 and CDs in the process of reuse. For visualization, there are some digital photos for the degradation of IC under different experimental conditions (Fig. 4d, e and f). It can be seen that the catalytic reaction of IC can complete within 4.5 and 9 min with and without sunlight and in dark with the solutions varying from blue to yellowish. Meanwhile, the overall ecotoxicity of the degradation products in the treated IC solution is far lower than that of the corresponding untreated IC solution (2 mg/mL). As shown in Fig. S5, after 3 days of cultivation, the growth of mung bean sprouts is obviously different. The length of bean sprouts in IC solution is significantly shorter than that in tap water and IC solution after degradation (Fig. S5a). After 5 days of cultivation, although they are basically the same length, due to the effect of dyes on bean sprouts, its growth is obviously different in deionized water and IC solution treated (Fig. S5b). For comparison with other methods, the materials used and results of dye degradation are listed in Table 1 . It can be observed that the degradation time is shortest using the present CDs products. Furthermore, it is the first time to report there is a fast degradation reaction of dye in dark using CDs as catalysts.

Surface hole transport plays the role in photocatalysis. Because many surface groups including CO [42] (Figs. 1b and 2b) can give rise to the high upward band bending of CDs. CDs have more surface holes transport, bringing about high photocatalytic activity. Such as an effective photogenerated e−-h+ pair separation, the process can accelerate photocatalysis. When CDs were irradiated the photons, and then CDs will be excited by the photons, whose energy is higher than the energy band of CDs, as shown in Eq. (1), the electron transfer from ground state to excited state. These separated electrons and holes migrate to the surface of CDs, and it will accelerate the production of ·OH [43] and ·O2 − [44]. It is obvious that they can degrade organic dyes in terms of the previous report [43]. The possible mechanism of CDs is shown in Fig. 5 for catalytic degradation of IC.
(2)
CDs + hv →
e e
+
h +

In order to further elucidate the influence of the major active species in the catalytic process, three scavengers were selected to eliminate the related active major species in the catalytic process. As a contrast, a control experiment without scavengers was achieved under the same conditions. The catalytic efficiency is shown in Fig. 6 . According to literatures [44,45], ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH play important roles in the catalytic process, and BQ, Na2-EDTA, and IPA were employed to remove ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6b and c, there are electrons transiting from ground state to excited state without sunlight and in dark indicating the formation of product ·OH and ·O2 −. Because CDs are excellent electron donors, the surface of CDs has surplus electrons. In the presence of hydrogen peroxide, hydrogen peroxide combines with electrons, two species, ·OH and ·O2 −, are responsible for the degradation of dye molecules under dark conditions [46]. However, sunlight will accelerate the transition of electrons. This is why the rate of photocatalysis is faster than that of catalysis without sunlight, and in dark.

In summary, CDs as a metal-free nanocatalyst was proposed to be used in the photocatalysis of dye degradation. CDs were prepared using a simple solvothermal approach from glyoxal and ethanol. CDs show excellent fluorescence as well as photocatalysis properties besides their easy operation and low cost. With the auxiliary of H2O2, CDs can make IC degrade effectively within 4.5 min, which is shorter than that of previous work. Furthermore, there exists degradation of IC without light in the system of CDs-H2O2. The photocatalysis efficiency reaches 91.2% and 91.5% with and without sunlight, and it also gets to 91.5% within 9 min even in dark. The rate constant of photocatalysis reaction is 0.553 min−1, 0.2258 min−1, and 0.2432 min−1 with and without sunlight, and in dark, respectively. The present catalytic system without metal catalysis avoids the secondary pollution of metal ions. Therefore, the CDs + H2O2 system has great application potential in pollutant treatment.

Shuai Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaoyu Fan: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis. Rentian Guan: Data curation, Resources, Validation. Yingying Hu: Visualization, Resources. Shuhan Jiang: Visualization, Resources. Xiaodong Shao: Supervision, Resources. Shuhao Wang: Supervision, Validation. Qiaoli Yue: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by Natural Science Foundation of China (91543206) and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022).
